sections.t: A comprehensive, interlinked knowledge base on the India Pale Ale — from its contested 18th century origins to the haze clouded, thiol driven frontier of modern brewing. Written for the intellectually curious: equally useful to a first time drinker and a brewmaster of forty years. [!tip] New here? Start with What Is an IPA, skim the IPA Family Tree, then read How to Use This Knowledge Base.

Domain Start at What you'll find 📜 History History of IPAs Origins, myths, the craft revival, and where the style is heading 🗺️ Styles IPA Styles Every IPA sub style, mapped — see the IPA Style Comparison Table 🌿 Ingredients Ingredients Hops, malt, yeast, water — the four pillars of IPA character 🧪 Brewing Guide Brewing Guide The full process, recipe formulation, and worked recipes 🔬 Science & Sensory Science and Sensory Bitterness chemistry, Biotransformation, haze, off flavors 🍻 Drinking an IPA Drinking an IPA Glassware, freshness, pairing, and regional drinking culture 🏆 Best IPAs Best IPAs Curated best of lists by style and region 🏭 Industry & Culture Industry and Culture Breweries, people, business, and beer culture 📚 Reference Reference Glossary of IPA Terms, Master Index, Timeline of IPA History

An IPA is a Hops forward Beer Flavor Wheel defined by assertive bitterness, aroma, and flavor. Born of British Origins of Pale Ale tradition and reborn in the The American Craft Beer Revolution, it has fractured into dozens of IPA Styles — the crisp, bitter West Coast IPA; the soft, juicy New England IPA; the hop saturated Double IPA; and frontier experiments like the Cold IPA and Brut IPA. More than any other beer, the IPA is the canvas on which modern brewing innovation is painted.

🆕 Just getting into beer → What Is an IPA · How to Taste an IPA 🍳 Want to brew one → Brewing Guide · Recipe Formulation 🛒 Want to drink the best → Best IPAs · IPA Freshness and Shelf Life 🤓 Want the deep science → Science and Sensory · Thiols and Hop Burst

IPA stands for India Pale Ale — a hop forward style of Origins of Pale Ale characterized by pronounced bitterness, hop flavor, and hop aroma, typically balanced over a relatively light, dry malt base. It is the best selling craft beer style in the United States and the defining beer of the modern craft movement.

An IPA is recognizable by four traits, all of which trace back to Hops: 1. Bitterness — measured in IBU and Perceived Bitterness, from a restrained 40 in a Session IPA to 100+ in a Double IPA. 2. Hop aroma & flavor — citrus, pine, tropical fruit, resin, floral, and dank notes, driven by Hop Oils and Terpenes and Thiols and Hop Burst. 3. A supporting malt base — usually pale and dry, designed to let hops lead. See Base Malts. 4. Moderate to high strength — most sit between 5.5% and 7.5% ABV, though the style spans Session IPA to Triple IPA.

Not simply "a strong beer" — strength is incidental; hops are the point. Not always bitter — a New England IPA can be aromatic yet mild in bitterness. Not a single recipe — the IPA is a family of styles. See the IPA Family Tree.

The "India" in India Pale Ale reflects the style's (heavily mythologized) association with British beer exported to colonial India in the 1800s. The popular story — that extra hops and alcohol were added to survive the sea voyage — is largely a legend . The real history is more interesting and is told in The October Beer Myth and Hodgson and the East India Trade.

No beer style has evolved faster. The IPA has, in roughly two centuries, gone from a English IPA to a West Coast IPA to a New England IPA — and continues to spin off novelties like the Brut IPA, Cold IPA, and Milkshake IPA. Understanding the IPA means understanding a moving target.
The IPA is not one beer but a family descended from a single ancestor. This note maps the lineage; the IPA Styles MOC and IPA Style Comparison Table cover each branch in detail.

1. The Ancestor — English Pale Ale → English IPA Earthy, marmalade like English hops over a biscuity malt base; balanced, moderate. The historical root — see Origins of Pale Ale. 2. The American Reinvention — American IPA The The American Craft Beer Revolution swapped English hops for punchy American varieties like Cascade and Centennial. This split into two dominant modern poles: West Coast IPA — clear, dry, aggressively bitter, citrus and pine. New England IPA — hazy, soft, low bitterness, saturated tropical aroma. 3. The Frontier — Specialty & Experimental Each modern pole spawned offshoots: Branch Notable offshoots Strength Double IPA, Triple IPA, Session IPA Color / malt Black IPA, Red IPA, White IPA Process Cold IPA, Brut IPA, Double Dry Hopping\ Adjunct Milkshake IPA, Rye IPA Yeast Belgian IPA, Brett IPA, Sour IPA Cross genre India Pale Lager See Specialty and Experimental IPAs for the long tail and Modern IPA Diversification for how this explosion happened.

Two axes explain almost every IPA: 1. Clarity / mouthfeel — bitter & clear (West Coast lineage) ↔ soft & hazy (New England lineage). Driven by Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio and Hop Haze and Colloidal Stability. 2. Intensity — session → standard → double → triple, scaling Alpha Acids and Bitterness, malt, and Hops.

title: How to Use This Knowledge Base

sections.t: This vault is built for [Obsidian](https://obsidian.md) and is best explored as a web of linked notes rather than read front to back.

Notes are grouped into ten numbered folders, each a domain : Folder Domain Hub note Orientation Home History History of IPAs Styles IPA Styles Ingredients Ingredients Brewing Brewing Guide Science Science and Sensory Drinking Drinking an IPA Best of lists Best IPAs Industry Industry and Culture Reference Reference

MOCs (Maps of Content) — each domain has a hub note that links to everything inside it. Start there. The top level hub is Home. Wikilinks — connect related concepts across domains. Follow them freely; that is the point. Graph view — open Obsidian's graph ( ) to see the whole web. Dense clusters reveal core concepts like Hops and Biotransformation. Tags — every note carries and topical tags in its frontmatter. Search or to slice across folders. Backlinks — the backlinks panel shows every note that references the one you're reading — often the fastest way to discover related material.

[!example] The Newcomer What Is an IPA → IPA Family Tree → How to Taste an IPA → Glassware for IPAs → Best IPAs [!example] The Aspiring Brewer Brewing Guide → Recipe Formulation → Hop Additions and Timing → Dry Hopping → West Coast IPA Recipe [!example] The Beer Geek Hop Chemistry → Biotransformation → Thiols and Hop Burst → Dry Hop Creep Explained → The Future of IPA [!example] The Historian History of IPAs → The October Beer Myth → Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale → Rise of the West Coast IPA

This knowledge base reflects brewing knowledge and consensus history. Beer history is genuinely contested — where a popular story is myth, the notes say so (see The October Beer Myth). Tasting notes and "best of" picks are informed opinion, not absolute fact. Use Recommended Reading and Resources to go deeper.

The India Pale Ale has one of the most misunderstood histories in beer. Its story is not a single invention but a two and a half century chain of accidents, trade economics, water chemistry, taxation, and — twice — outright reinvention. This note is the hub for the History domain; every era below has its own dedicated note.

Most of what casual drinkers "know" about IPA is wrong. The style was not designed for a sea voyage, was not unusually strong for its era, and was not even called "IPA" when it first sailed to India. Separating fact from folklore — see The October Beer Myth — is the first job of any honest IPA history.

Era Roughly What happened Key note Pre history 1700s Coke fired pale malt makes pale ale possible Origins of Pale Ale The export era 1780s–1820s London brewers ship pale ale to India Hodgson and the East India Trade The Burton boom 1820s–1880s Burton's sulfate water perfects the style Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale Imperial spread 1800s IPA follows the British flag worldwide IPA in the British Empire The long decline 1880s–1970s War, tax, and lager hollow the style out Decline of IPA in Britain American seeds 1600s–1970s Pale ale crosses the Atlantic IPA in Early America The craft revival 1965–1990 Microbreweries reignite hop forward beer The American Craft Beer Revolution The modern prototype 1975 Anchor dry hops with Cascade Anchor Liberty Ale and the First Modern IPA West Coast era 1990s–2000s Bitterness as identity Rise of the West Coast IPA The strength race 2000s Imperial escalation Origins of the Double IPA The haze era 2010s Soft, juicy, turbid IPA The New England IPA Emergence Global & fragmented 2010s–now Worldwide spread, endless sub styles Globalization of IPA, Modern IPA Diversification The frontier now → Thiols, low alcohol, sustainability The Future of IPA [!note] Two births, one name The IPA effectively has two origin stories : a British one (export pale ale, 1780s–1880s) and an American one (craft revival, 1975 onward). The modern IPA owes far more to the second than the first. The name survived; the recipe did not.

No other beer has been declared "dead" and then resurrected as a market leader. The British IPA faded to a weak supermarket label by the mid 20th century. American brewers, lacking the cultural baggage, treated "IPA" as a blank canvas for Hops expression — and in doing so created something the Victorians would barely recognize. For the dates in one place, see the Timeline of IPA History.
Before there was an What Is an IPA, there was simply pale ale — and pale ale could not exist until brewers learned how to make pale Malt. The IPA's deepest root is not a recipe but a fuel: coke .

For most of brewing history, malt was kilned over wood, straw, or peat fires. Direct, smoky flames produced malt that was dark, scorched, and often tainted with smoke flavor. The beer made from it was correspondingly brown or black. A genuinely pale malt — and therefore a pale beer — was technically out of reach. [!note] Pale meant expensive Early pale malt required gentler, more controlled heat, which meant more fuel and more skill. Pale ale began life as a premium product , not a default one.

In the early 1600s, English maltsters began using coke — coal baked to drive off its smoke and impurities, much as charcoal is made from wood. Coke burned cleanly and at a controllable temperature, allowing malt to be dried pale without scorching or smoke taint. The technique is often associated with Derbyshire maltsters and spread through the 1600s and 1700s. The result was the first reliably pale malt , and with it, genuinely pale beer.

sections.h: The Rise of 18th Century Pale Ale
sections.t: By the 1700s, English brewers were producing a range of pale ales and "October beers" — strong, well hopped ales brewed in autumn from the freshest Noble and English Hops and barley, then aged for months or years. These were prestige beers of the landed gentry and the urban middle class. Feature 18th century pale ale Modern IPA Malt Pale, coke kilned Pale Base Malts Hops Generous, English, for preservation Generous, global, for aroma Conditioning Long cellar aging Fresh, hop aroma driven Purpose Prestige & keeping quality Hop showcase This pale ale tradition — strong, hoppy, pale, and built to age — is the direct ancestor of the beer that would soon be shipped east. The hopping rates were high not as a marketing gimmick but because Hops are a natural preservative, and a beer meant to keep needed them. [!tip] The IPA was an evolution , not an invention No single person "invented" the IPA. It emerged from an existing class of strong, pale, well hopped English ales. What changed was the market for them — see Hodgson and the East India Trade.

Crucially, the term "India Pale Ale" did not exist in the 1700s. Brewers spoke of "pale ale prepared for the India market" or "October beer." The branded name came later, in the 1830s–1840s, once the export trade was established and competitive. The drink predates its famous label by half a century.

Ask almost any drinker why an IPA is hoppy and strong, and you will hear the same story: brewers loaded pale ale with extra hops and extra alcohol so it would survive the long sea voyage to India . It is a tidy, satisfying tale. It is also, in its popular form, mostly a myth . [!warning] The popular legend "The IPA was invented as a high alcohol, heavily hopped beer specifically engineered to survive a voyage that would have spoiled an ordinary beer." — This is not what happened.

The beer shipped to India descended from October beer — a class of strong, pale, well hopped English ales brewed in the autumn (when fresh barley and Noble and English Hops were available) and matured for many months. See Origins of Pale Ale. October beer was already strong and already well hopped — not for India, but because that is simply what a prestige keeping ale was. When this beer began going to India, brewers did not have to invent anything. They shipped a beer that already existed.

Claim Reality "Extra alcohol was added for the voyage" Export pale ale was a normal strength for a keeping ale; some India shipments were actually weaker than domestic stock ales. "Extra hops were added for the voyage" Hopping rates were high because Hops preserve beer — true of all keeping ales, not a special India recipe. "Ordinary beer couldn't survive the trip" Porter and other beers were exported successfully too. India received a wide range of British beer. "The IPA was invented for India" It evolved from existing October/pale ale; the name "IPA" came decades later.

A few elements of the legend hold up: The sea voyage did affect the beer. Months of motion, heat crossing the equator twice, and temperature swings accelerated maturation — the beer often arrived unusually well conditioned, bright, and "ripe." This was a genuine and prized effect. Pale ale did travel and sell well in India, and a market reputation grew around it. Hops are a preservative, so a well hopped beer travelled better than a lightly hopped one. So the voyage shaped the beer's character — it did not dictate its invention . [!example] How the myth spread The "engineered for the voyage" story is a 20th century simplification, repeated on countless beer labels and bar chalkboards because it makes good marketing. Beer historians (notably Martyn Cornell and Ron Pattinson) have documented the brewing records that contradict it.

Believing the myth leads to a wrong model of the style: that IPA means "strong and bitter for its own sake." In truth, the defining trait was always hop character on a pale base , and strength was incidental — a point made again in What Is an IPA. The myth also obscures the real drivers of IPA's success: trade economics (Hodgson and the East India Trade) and water chemistry (Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale).

title: Hodgson and the East India Trade

sections.t: If any single brewer is associated with the birth of the IPA, it is George Hodgson of the Bow Brewery in East London. But Hodgson did not invent a beer — he exploited a trade route . Understanding how he did it explains far more about the IPA than any recipe.

sections.h: The Right Brewery in the Right Place
sections.t: The Bow Brewery sat on the River Lea in Bromley by Bow , close to the docks at Blackwall where ships of the East India Company loaded for the long voyage east. Geography did much of Hodgson's work for him: his beer simply did not have far to travel before it was on a ship. [!note] He shipped pale ale — and porter Hodgson's export trade included Origins of Pale Ale and dark porter. The notion that he specialised in a single voyage proof "IPA" is a later simplification.

Hodgson's real advantage was commercial, not technical : Lever What Hodgson did Credit Offered the East India Company's officers generous credit — long terms before payment was due. Logistics Sold directly to ships' captains and officers, who carried beer as a private trading venture. Location Brewed beside the loading docks, minimising handling and cost. Reputation Built a near monopoly on the India beer trade by the early 1800s. The officers and captains who carried the beer could sell it at a steep markup in India, where good British beer was scarce and prized by colonial administrators, soldiers, and merchants.

sections.h: The Voyage as a Maturation Tank
sections.t: The months long passage — out through the Atlantic, across the equator twice, around the Cape of Good Hope — subjected the beer to constant motion and heat. This accelerated its conditioning, and well hopped pale ale tended to arrive bright, ripe, and clarified . It was a genuine quality effect, and it built the style's reputation. But it was a consequence of the trade, not its purpose — see The October Beer Myth.

By the 1820s, Hodgson's heirs grew greedy. They tried to cut out the captains and control distribution themselves, raised prices, and manipulated supply. They alienated the East India Company. [!example] The opening for Burton Frustrated, the East India Company's Samuel Allsopp reportedly approached Burton on Trent brewers to break Hodgson's grip. The Burton brewers — with their extraordinary water — would soon outclass him entirely. See Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale.

Hodgson did not create the IPA recipe. What he did was create the market : he proved that pale ale could be a profitable export commodity and established the India trade as a recognised, branded category. Within a generation, "pale ale as prepared for India" became "India Pale Ale" — a name born of marketing once competition arrived. His story is a centrepiece of Key Figures in IPA History.
If Hodgson and the East India Trade created the market for export pale ale, the brewers of Burton on Trent perfected the beer. They did so thanks to one largely invisible ingredient: their water .

Burton on Trent, a small town in Staffordshire, sits above gypsum rich strata. Its well water is exceptionally hard, loaded with calcium sulfate (gypsum) and other mineral salts. Ion Effect on pale ale Sulfate Sharpens and accentuates hop bitterness; gives a crisp, dry finish Calcium Aids mash chemistry, clarity, and yeast health Low carbonate Allows a low mash pH, ideal for a pale grain bill The result is a beer in which Hops bitterness is clean, firm, and bright rather than coarse — the so called " Burton snatch ," a faint sulfurous note famously likened to struck matches. For the chemistry, see Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio. [!note] The accidental advantage Burton brewers did not understand the science — that came later. They simply found their water made spectacular pale ale. Burton water remains the template for West Coast IPA brewers today.

When Hodgson's Bow Brewery overreached in the 1820s, the East India Company turned to Burton. Brewers including Allsopp , Bass , and Salt began producing pale ale "as prepared for the India market." Their beer was simply better — crisper and more drinkable — and Burton rapidly overtook London in the India trade. Bass became one of the most famous breweries in the world; its red triangle is often cited as the UK's first registered trademark (1876). The railways, arriving in the 1830s–40s, let Burton ship cheaply across Britain, not just to the docks.

sections.h: From Export Beer to National Sensation
sections.t: Crucially, Burton pale ale did not stay an export product. As it became available domestically — by rail, in bottle, and on draught — British drinkers discovered it for themselves. India Pale Ale became a hugely fashionable beer at home , sold in pubs across the country through the mid 1800s. The "India" in the name was, by then, as much a prestige label as a literal destination. [!example] Burtonisation So desirable was Burton water that brewers elsewhere began adding gypsum and other salts to mimic it — a practice called Burtonisation . It is the direct ancestor of modern Water Treatment for Brewing, and every brewer who salts their liquor for an IPA is following Burton's lead.

Burton on Trent established the quality benchmark for IPA and proved that a beer's character could hinge on local geology. When American craft brewers revived the hop forward IPA over a century later, they reached — knowingly — for high sulfate, "Burton style" water. The town's influence outlived its export trade by generations.

By the mid 1800s, India Pale Ale was no longer just a beer "for India." It had become the prestige export ale of the entire British Empire , following soldiers, administrators, merchants, and settlers across the globe.

sections.h: A Beer That Travelled the Trade Routes
sections.t: The same qualities that suited pale ale to the India voyage — a pale, dry malt base and a generous, preservative hop charge — made it ideal for any long passage. Where the Royal Navy sailed and the Empire planted a flag, Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale pale ale tended to follow. Market Notes India The original and most famous market; the prestige drink of the British in India Australia & New Zealand Settler colonies with a strong thirst for British beer The Caribbean & West Indies Established trade routes carried pale ale alongside porter Canada & North America British pale ale exported and, increasingly, brewed locally South Africa & the Cape A natural stop on the India route [!note] Not the only beer that travelled Porter, stout, and "Burton ale" all sailed the Empire too. IPA was the prestige export ale, but it never held a monopoly. The myth that only IPA could survive a sea voyage is firmly dismantled in The October Beer Myth.

Three forces pushed IPA across the world in the 19th century: 1. Steam and rail. Faster ships and the Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale railways slashed transport cost and time, widening the market. 2. Bottling. Improved bottling let pale ale reach drinkers far from any brewery in stable, branded form — see Reading an IPA Label for how branding later evolved. 3. Imperial prestige. Drinking British pale ale was a marker of cultural identity for Britons abroad — a "taste of home."

The Empire did not only receive IPA; it began to make it. Breweries in Australia, India, Canada, and elsewhere brewed their own pale ales, often consciously imitating the Burton style. This established local brewing traditions that, in some cases, outlasted the British export trade itself — an early preview of the worldwide spread covered in Globalization of IPA. [!example] The high water mark The mid to late 19th century was the peak of British IPA: fashionable at home, dominant across the Empire, and synonymous with the prestige of Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale. Almost nothing about that supremacy would survive the next century.

Even at its height, forces were gathering that would undo British IPA: rising taxation , the disruption of two world wars, and the relentless advance of pale lager as the global beer of choice. Within a few generations, "IPA" in Britain would mean a weak, ordinary supermarket bitter. That collapse is the subject of Decline of IPA in Britain.

The IPA's Victorian peak did not last. Over roughly a century — from the 1880s to the 1970s — British India Pale Ale shrank from a prestige export sensation to a thin, forgettable label on a supermarket shelf. Understanding why explains why the style's revival happened in America , not Britain.

sections.h: A Slow Collapse, Not a Single Blow
sections.t: No one event killed British IPA. It was eroded by a sequence of pressures. Force Effect on IPA Taxation by gravity From 1880, beer was taxed on its strength, creating a permanent incentive to brew weaker beer World War I Grain restrictions, reduced output, and sharply higher duty slashed average beer strength World War II Further rationing of grain and a depressed economy locked in weak beer The rise of lager Cold, pale, mass marketed lager steadily captured the mainstream drinker Brewery consolidation Mergers favoured bland, standardised national brands over distinctive regional ales [!warning] Tax shaped the recipe The single most important factor was tax on alcoholic strength . Once a brewer paid more duty for a stronger beer, "IPA" had a structural reason to get weaker every decade. By the mid 20th century, a British "IPA" might be a 3–3.5% ABV pale bitter — a world away from its Victorian ancestor.

By the 1950s–60s, "India Pale Ale" survived in Britain mainly as a marketing label for cheap, low gravity bitter. The hop character, the strength, and the prestige were gone. Many drinkers encountered "IPA" only as an inexpensive supermarket or pub bitter brand and had no idea it had ever meant anything else. Meanwhile, the global tide ran toward pale lager , which the big consolidated brewers promoted heavily. The hoppy English pale ale tradition did not vanish entirely — it survived as cask bitter , championed later by the Campaign for Real Ale — but the IPA name had been hollowed out. [!note] A style declared dead By the 1970s, serious beer writers could reasonably describe the historic IPA as effectively extinct . This is the crucial setup for the style's revival: when American brewers picked up the name, it carried almost no living tradition or expectation.

Because British "IPA" had become a weak, generic label, it offered American craft brewers a blank canvas . They were not reviving a beer their grandparents drank; they were reinterpreting a historical idea — pale, hoppy ale — with new American Hops and no cultural baggage. See The American Craft Beer Revolution and Anchor Liberty Ale and the First Modern IPA. [!example] Irony of the comeback Decades later, the American style IPA would be exported back to Britain , where it helped spark the UK's own craft revival — the style returning home almost unrecognisable. See Globalization of IPA.

The American IPA story is usually told as starting in the 1970s — but pale ale crossed the Atlantic long before that. Understanding the pre craft centuries explains why, when the revival came, "IPA" was an empty vessel ready to be refilled.

Colonial America was, broadly, an ale drinking society. English settlers brought English brewing traditions, and ale, cider, and rum were dietary staples. Pale ale existed, but on a modest scale; brewing was often domestic or small and local, and quality varied. [!note] Imported and imitated Through the 18th and early 19th centuries, Americans both imported British pale ale and brewed local versions . Some early American breweries explicitly advertised "India Pale Ale" or "pale ale" in the 1800s, riding the prestige of the British style described in IPA in the British Empire.

American pale ale and "India ale" had a real, if regional, presence in the 1800s — particularly in the Northeast, where English brewing influence was strongest. But two forces reshaped American beer: Force Effect German immigration Mid 1800s German immigrants brought lager , which rapidly overtook ale Industrial brewing Refrigeration, rail, and pasteurisation favoured pale lager at national scale By the late 19th century, American beer was overwhelmingly lager . Ale, including any "India pale" styles, became a minor footnote.

[!warning] 1920–1933 National Prohibition shut down the American brewing industry for thirteen years. When it ended, the survivors were large lager brewers. Small, traditional, and ale focused breweries had largely been wiped out — a near total break with any older American IPA tradition. After Repeal, the surviving industry consolidated relentlessly. By the mid 20th century, American beer meant a handful of giant brands producing light, mild, adjunct laden lager. A hoppy pale ale was nowhere to be found on the mainstream market.

This near total erasure is, paradoxically, the key to the modern IPA. By the 1960s: The British IPA had decayed into a weak label — see Decline of IPA in Britain. The American IPA tradition had been broken by lager's rise and Prohibition. So when a new generation of American brewers reached for "India Pale Ale," they inherited a name with no living recipe in either country. There was no orthodoxy to honour and no consumer expectation to satisfy. That freedom is exactly what made the explosive reinvention possible. [!example] The vessel was empty The IPA's American rebirth was not a continuation of early American brewing — it was a fresh interpretation built on near zero living memory. The story picks up with The American Craft Beer Revolution.

The modern IPA exists because of a broader upheaval: the American craft beer revolution , a roughly thirty year transformation that took the United States from a near monopoly of light lager to the most diverse beer market on Earth.

sections.h: The Starting Point: A Beer Desert
sections.t: By the late 1960s, American beer was dominated by a handful of giant brewers making light, mild adjunct lager. As IPA in Early America explains, Prohibition and consolidation had erased almost all stylistic diversity. There was, effectively, one kind of beer .

Year Event Why it mattered 1965 Fritz Maytag buys the failing Anchor Brewing in San Francisco Saves a traditional ale brewery; a model for "small and characterful" 1976 New Albion Brewing founded in California Widely cited as the first new American microbrewery from scratch 1978 Federal legalization of homebrewing Created the talent pipeline for nearly every future craft brewer 1980s Sierra Nevada, Boston Beer, and others launch Proved craft beer could be a real business [!note] Why homebrewing legalization mattered so much President Carter signed the bill legalizing home brewing (effective 1979). Almost every influential American craft brewer of the next decades — including the pioneers of the Origins of the Double IPA and the The New England IPA Emergence — started as a homebrewer . The law turned a hobby into an industry's training ground. See Homebrewing an IPA.

Fritz Maytag's rescue of Anchor Brewing is the symbolic start. He restored quality, embraced traditional all malt brewing, and in 1975 produced Liberty Ale — the beer now widely regarded as the prototype of the American IPA. That single beer gets its own note: Anchor Liberty Ale and the First Modern IPA.

Through the 1980s and 1990s, hundreds — then thousands — of small breweries opened. They differentiated themselves with flavor , and the most reliable way to deliver flavor was Hops. American Cascade, with their bold citrus pine character, became the signature of the movement. [!example] Hops as the craft signature Big lager was defined by restraint . Craft beer defined itself by the opposite — and the IPA, as the most hop forward style, became craft beer's banner. To drink an IPA was to make a statement against bland mass market lager.

By the 2000s, craft beer was no longer a rebellion but an industry — with its own The Craft Beer Industry, Beer Distribution and the Three Tier System, and Landmark IPA Breweries. And at its commercial heart sat the IPA, which would go on to become the best selling craft style in America. The next chapters — Rise of the West Coast IPA and beyond — are all built on this foundation.

title: Anchor Liberty Ale and the First Modern IPA

sections.t: If the modern IPA has a single point of origin, most historians place it in 1975 , in San Francisco, with one beer: Anchor Liberty Ale . It was not labelled an "IPA" — but it established the template that every American IPA has followed since.

Liberty Ale was brewed by Anchor Brewing under The American Craft Beer Revolution to commemorate the 200th anniversary of Paul Revere's ride (1775 → 1975). It was a pale, assertive ale that did two things almost no contemporary American beer was doing: Feature Why it was revolutionary Brewed entirely with Cascade hops Cascade was a brand new American variety with bold citrus floral character Dry hopped Whole cone hops added after fermentation for pure aroma — a revived English technique Pale, dry, all malt base Let the hops lead, with no adjunct or heavy malt to muddy them [!note] The Cascade moment Cascade, released by the USDA breeding program in 1972, gave American brewers a hop unlike anything English. Liberty Ale was its showcase. The grapefruit and pine signature of the American IPA — see Hop Oils and Terpenes — effectively begins here.

Liberty Ale was sold simply as "ale." The term "IPA" was attached later, retrospectively. But in its construction , it is unmistakably the prototype: Pale, lean malt base built as a stage for hops. Aggressive American hop flavor and aroma. Dry Hopping used deliberately for aroma intensity. Every defining trait of the American IPA is present. What changed afterward was degree — more hops, more bitterness, higher strength — not concept . [!example] Prototype, not a fully formed style Liberty Ale is the missing link : more hop forward than an English pale ale, less extreme than a 1990s West Coast IPA. It is to the IPA roughly what an early ancestor is to a modern species — recognisably the same lineage, visibly earlier on the branch.

Dry Hopping — adding hops after the boil for aroma without bitterness — was an old English keeping ale practice that had largely fallen out of American use. Anchor's deliberate dry hopping of Liberty Ale reintroduced the technique to American craft brewing. It is now utterly central to the IPA, from the West Coast IPA to the Double Dry Hopping New England IPA.

Liberty Ale demonstrated three things that the entire craft IPA movement would build on: 1. A single, characterful American hop could define a beer. 2. Aroma — not just bitterness — was worth chasing. 3. American drinkers would embrace an intensely hoppy beer. Sierra Nevada Pale Ale (1980), another Cascade driven landmark, soon reinforced the lesson. From there the path runs straight to Rise of the West Coast IPA. Liberty Ale earns its place among the Iconic IPAs That Defined the Style.

title: Rise of the West Coast IPA

sections.t: If Anchor Liberty Ale and the First Modern IPA was the prototype, the West Coast IPA was the style fully formed. Through the 1990s and 2000s, American brewers — overwhelmingly on the Pacific coast — turned the IPA into something loud, bitter, and proudly extreme.

The West Coast IPA was not just a recipe; it was a statement . In a market still dominated by mild lager, brewers used aggressive Hops bitterness as a badge of identity — a beer that announced it was not for everyone. Trait West Coast IPA Bitterness High — often 60–80+ IBU and Perceived Bitterness Malt Pale, dry, lean — gets out of the hops' way Body Light to medium, crisp finish Hop character Citrus, pine, resin, "dank" — see Hop Oils and Terpenes Appearance Brilliantly clear, gold to amber Water High sulfate, "Burton style" — see Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio [!note] Bitterness as identity Where the English IPA sought balance , the West Coast IPA sought contrast . A bracing, lingering bitterness was the whole point — and drinkers' tolerance for it became a kind of craft beer initiation.

A cluster of breweries defined the style: Stone Brewing (founded 1996, San Diego) — Stone IPA and the deliberately confrontational Arrogant Bastard made aggression a brand. Russian River (Sonoma County) — Vinnie Cilurzo's hop forward beers, soon including the era defining Origins of the Double IPA. Bell's Brewery (Michigan — not the coast, but spiritually aligned) — Two Hearted Ale, a Centennial driven IPA beloved nationwide. AleSmith, Ballast Point, Green Flash, Lagunitas and many more amplified the template. San Diego in particular became synonymous with the style. See Landmark IPA Breweries.

The West Coast IPA rode a wave of new American Hop Growing Regions: Centennial, Simcoe, Amarillo, and later Citra. These varieties delivered the citrus pine resin punch the style demanded. The escalating hop arms race also drove brewers toward ever higher strength — leading directly to the Origins of the Double IPA. [!example] The IBU arms race For roughly a decade, breweries competed on IBU numbers as a marketing tool, advertising ever higher bitterness. The arms race eventually hit a wall — the human palate can only perceive so much bitterness (see IBU and Perceived Bitterness) — and the next era would react against it.

By the mid 2010s, the bracingly bitter West Coast IPA was partly eclipsed by the soft, hazy New England IPA. But it never disappeared, and the late 2010s–2020s brought a deliberate West Coast revival — drier, cleaner, lower bitterness reinterpretations that kept the clarity and pine while easing the harshness. The style remains a craft cornerstone; see West Coast IPA for the modern profile.

As the Rise of the West Coast IPA pushed bitterness ever higher in the 1990s, brewers ran into a problem of balance — and the solution was simply to make the beer bigger . The result was the Double IPA (or Imperial IPA), and its origin story has an unusually clear point of birth.

sections.h: Vinnie Cilurzo and the First Double IPA
sections.t: The Double IPA is widely credited to brewer Vinnie Cilurzo , at the Blind Pig Brewing Company in Temecula, California, around 1994 . The often told story is candid and practical: [!example] An origin born of necessity Cilurzo was working with a worn out brewhouse and worried his very hoppy beer would taste harsh and unbalanced. His fix was to increase the malt — more malt meant more alcohol and more body to carry an enormous hop load. He has described it, with some humour, as a way to mask the limitations of his equipment. A near disaster became a new style.

Cilurzo later moved to Russian River Brewing , where he refined the idea into Pliny the Elder — released in 2000 and named (with a wink) after the Roman naturalist associated with naming the hop plant. Pliny became the archetype of the Double IPA : intensely hoppy yet startlingly drinkable, dry rather than sweet, and balanced despite its strength. Feature Double IPA archetype ABV 8–10%+ Bitterness Very high IBU and Perceived Bitterness, but balanced Malt More than a standard IPA, but kept dry Hops Massive charges, often including late and Dry Hopping additions Finish Crisp and clean — not sticky or sweet Russian River's later Pliny the Younger , a Triple IPA, pushed the escalation even further and became a cult release. See Iconic IPAs That Defined the Style.

The terms borrow from older brewing language — "imperial" historically denoted a stronger, export grade beer (as in imperial stout). Applied to the IPA, "double" and "imperial" signal a bigger version of the standard style : more malt, more hops, more alcohol. The naming is loose; there is no strict doubling of anything. [!note] The key to a good Double IPA: dryness The hardest part of a Double IPA is keeping it from becoming sweet and heavy. Skilled brewers use highly fermentable worts and careful Recipe Formulation so the beer finishes dry , letting the hops — not residual sugar — carry the strength. A poorly made Double IPA is cloying; a great one drinks dangerously easy.

The Double IPA legitimised the idea that IPAs could keep getting bigger , opening the door to the Triple IPA and an enduring craft beer fascination with strength. It also set the stage for the next twist — the The New England IPA Emergence, many of whose flagship beers are themselves Double IPAs.

By the early 2010s, the IPA had spent two decades chasing bitterness . Then a handful of brewers in the northeastern United States flipped the formula entirely — and the New England IPA (NEIPA), soft and hazy and bursting with juicy aroma, became the defining beer of the 2010s.

The NEIPA was, in part, a rebellion against the Rise of the West Coast IPA. Where the West Coast style prized aggressive IBU and Perceived Bitterness and crystal clarity, the New England style prized the opposite: Trait West Coast IPA New England IPA Bitterness High, lingering Low, soft Appearance Brilliantly clear Opaque, "hazy" / turbid Mouthfeel Crisp, dry Pillowy, full, juicy Hop expression Pine, resin, citrus rind Tropical fruit, juice, "hop burst" Water High sulfate High chloride — see Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio

Three breweries are central to the origin story: The Alchemist (Vermont) — John Kimmich's Heady Topper , a Double IPA canned from 2011, is the foundational NEIPA. Famously instructed to be drunk straight from the can, it became the style's first cult object. Hill Farmstead (Vermont) — Shaun Hill's acclaimed, intensely aromatic hoppy ales reinforced the regional reputation. Tree House Brewing (Massachusetts) — turned the hazy IPA into a national pilgrimage destination. Trillium Brewing (Massachusetts) — helped define and commercialise the soft, juicy profile. [!note] Vermont and Massachusetts as the cradle The style is named for its birthplace. New England's brewers, drinkers, and a wave of The Hype Beer Phenomenon demand co created it within a few intense years.

The signature turbidity is no accident. It comes from a combination of techniques covered across the brewing domain: High protein grists using oats and wheat — see Specialty Malts and Adjuncts. Fruity, low flocculating IPA Yeast Strains. Massive late and Dry Hopping additions, often Double Dry Hopping, driving Biotransformation of hop compounds. Chloride forward water for a fuller, rounder mouthfeel. The haze itself is a Hop Haze and Colloidal Stability of proteins, polyphenols, and hop material. Whether haze causes flavor or merely accompanies it is debated — but the soft, juicy character is real and is driven heavily by Thiols and Hop Burst and Hop Aroma Compounds. [!warning] The fragility trade off NEIPAs are gorgeous fresh and fade fast. Their low bitterness and high hop oil load make them especially vulnerable to Hop Fade and Oxidation. Freshness is non negotiable — see IPA Freshness and Shelf Life.

The NEIPA did not replace the West Coast IPA; it split the family tree . Today both coexist as major branches of the IPA Family Tree, and the hazy style spawned its own descendants — most notably the dessert like Milkshake IPA. It also drove the Modern IPA Diversification of the late 2010s.

The IPA crossed the world once already, in the 19th century, on the trade routes of IPA in the British Empire. Its second global spread — the craft IPA , from the 1990s onward — has been faster, broader, and more transformative than the first.

The hop forward American IPA, born in The American Craft Beer Revolution, became the export template of a worldwide craft beer movement. As local brewers in country after country discovered American craft beer, the IPA — bold, distinctive, and unmistakably not mass market lager — was the style they most often adopted first. Region How IPA took hold United Kingdom The American IPA returned to its ancestral home, helping spark the UK craft scene; BrewDog became a global symbol Scandinavia "Gypsy"/contract brewers like Mikkeller spread hoppy beer across Europe Continental Europe Italy, Spain, Germany, and others built craft scenes with IPA at the centre Australia & New Zealand Local hops (Galaxy, Nelson Sauvin) made the region a hop powerhouse Latin America & Asia Fast growing craft scenes in Brazil, Mexico, Japan, China and beyond [!example] The IPA boomerang The most striking story is Britain's: the style its breweries once exported to the Empire , then let decline , came back as an American reinvention and reignited British craft brewing. See Decline of IPA in Britain.

Globalization is not just about where IPA is drunk — it reshaped where its key ingredient is grown and moved . The modern IPA depends on an international Hop Contracts and the Hop Supply Chain: Growing regions now span the US Pacific Northwest, Germany, the UK, Australia, New Zealand, and beyond — see Hop Growing Regions. A brewer in any country can build an IPA from Citra, Galaxy, and Nelson Sauvin hops in a single recipe. Hop Products and Formats made hops stable and shippable worldwide. [!note] Terroir goes global Just as wine grapes express place, hops express Hop Terroir. Globalization let brewers everywhere blend the terroir of multiple continents into one glass — a genuinely new possibility in beer history.

The IPA did not spread as a fixed recipe; each scene adapted it. Brewers reach for local hops , local water, and local taste. The result is a patchwork of regional interpretations rather than one global IPA — and that variety feeds the next chapter, Modern IPA Diversification. For a tour of the results, see Best IPAs by Region Europe and Best IPAs by Region Rest of World.

sections.h: Why It Spread So Far, So Fast
sections.t: Three factors explain the speed: 1. A ready made template. Newcomer brewers had a proven, exciting style to copy. 2. The internet. Recipes, techniques, and Untappd and Beer Rating Culture crossed borders instantly. 3. Cultural meaning. Worldwide, the IPA signalled craft, independence, and flavor against industrial lager — the same meaning it carried in America.

For most of its history the IPA was, broadly, one beer. Since the 2010s it has fractured into a dizzying array of sub styles — brut, cold, milkshake, sour, black, white, and more. This note explains what happened and, more importantly, why .

sections.h: From a Style to a Platform
sections.t: The decisive shift is conceptual: the IPA stopped being a fixed recipe and became a platform — a hop forward base that brewers freely modify. Once Rise of the West Coast IPA and The New England IPA Emergence established two legitimate, very different IPAs, the door was open: "IPA" now meant "a hoppy beer," and almost anything could be hoppy.

sections.h: A Field Guide to the Sub Styles
sections.t: Sub style Core idea Note Session IPA IPA flavor at low ABV low strength Black IPA IPA hopping + dark malt "Cascadian Dark Ale" White IPA IPA crossed with witbier wheat + spice Belgian IPA IPA with expressive Belgian yeast yeast driven Rye IPA rye malt for spice and crispness grain twist Red IPA amber/red malt depth under hops malt twist Brut IPA bone dry, champagne like enzyme driven Milkshake IPA lactose + fruit + vanilla, dessert like NEIPA descendant Cold IPA IPA fermented like a lager, crisp and dry lager yeast hybrid Brett IPA Brettanomyces funk meets hops wild yeast Sour IPA kettle sour acidity + juicy hops acidity + hops India Pale Lager IPA hopping on a lager base lager crossover See Specialty and Experimental IPAs for the long tail beyond even these. [!note] Not all sub styles survive Diversification is partly fashion . The Brut IPA flared up around 2018 and faded fast; the Milkshake IPA and Cold IPA have proven stickier. Treat the list as a snapshot of an evolving market, not a permanent taxonomy.

Five forces converged: 1. Market saturation. With thousands of breweries, an IPA had to be novel to stand out. Novelty became a survival strategy — see The Business of Brewing an IPA. 2. Rating culture. Platforms like Untappd and Beer Rating Culture rewarded constant new releases; breweries churned variants to keep "checking in" drinkers engaged. 3. The hype cycle. The The Hype Beer Phenomenon made limited, unusual releases commercially valuable. 4. Technical tools. New IPA Yeast Strains, enzymes, Hop Products and Formats, and adjuncts made once difficult beers easy to brew. 5. A permissive definition. Because "IPA" had no protected meaning, anything hoppy could claim the label — and the label sold. [!warning] When does it stop being an IPA? Critics argue that a lactose and vanilla Milkshake IPA or a lager fermented Cold IPA stretches the term past meaning. There is no authority to decide; the BJCP and Style Guidelines document trends but do not police the market. The IPA's name has become a flag of convenience .

Diversification gave drinkers extraordinary choice and kept the IPA culturally central. But it also diluted the term and contributed to "IPA fatigue" among some drinkers. The reaction — a back to basics revival of the clean West Coast IPA — is itself part of the same restless cycle.

The IPA has reinvented itself roughly once a decade — from Rise of the West Coast IPA to The New England IPA Emergence to Modern IPA Diversification. What comes next? This note maps the frontiers that brewers and hop scientists are actively pushing, while being honest that forecasting beer is guesswork . [!warning] A note of humility Nobody predicted the hazy IPA before it arrived. Treat what follows as current trajectories , not prophecy.

The most active research frontier is thiols — potent sulfur compounds responsible for intense passionfruit, guava, and white wine aromas, perceptible at vanishingly low concentrations. See Thiols and Hop Burst. Breeders are selecting hop varieties rich in thiol precursors . "Thiolised" or bioengineered yeast strains can release bound thiol precursors during fermentation via Biotransformation, unlocking aroma that hops alone cannot. The goal: dramatic tropical intensity from less hop material. Approach Mechanism Thiol rich hop varieties More free thiols and precursors in the cone Thiol releasing yeast Enzymes cleave bound precursors during fermentation Targeted Dry Hopping timing Maximise Biotransformation of precursors

sections.h: Frontier 2 — Low and No Alcohol IPA
sections.t: Health conscious and "sober curious" drinkers have made non alcoholic IPA one of the fastest growing segments. The technical challenge is real: alcohol contributes body and aroma carriage, so a 0.0% IPA must rebuild mouthfeel and hop expression by other means. Expect this category — alongside the Session IPA — to keep expanding.

The IPA is a resource hungry beer: it uses far more hops than other styles, and hops are water and energy intensive to grow and process. The sustainability agenda — see Sustainability in IPA Brewing — is reshaping the style: Water reuse and reduced brewery water ratios. High efficiency hop products — Cryo Hops and Lupulin Powder, extracts, and concentrated formats that deliver aroma with less plant matter and less Hop Fade and Oxidation. Climate pressure on hop farms. Heat and drought threaten traditional Hop Growing Regions, pushing breeding toward resilient varieties — a supply chain risk detailed in Hop Contracts and the Hop Supply Chain.

After a decade of maximalism, several currents point toward restraint and precision : The West Coast revival — drier, cleaner, lower bitterness reinterpretations of the West Coast IPA. "Cold" and crisp formats like the Cold IPA and India Pale Lager, prizing drinkability. Better analytics letting brewers hit aroma targets with fewer, smarter hop additions. [!example] The pendulum keeps swinging IPA history is a series of reactions: bitter provoked hazy; maximal provoked the brut and the lean West Coast revival. The safest prediction is that whatever dominates next will eventually provoke its own opposite.

Through every reinvention, one thing has held since Origins of Pale Ale: the IPA is, fundamentally, a showcase for Hops . The vehicle changes; the destination does not. As long as brewers and drinkers want to taste the hop, the IPA — by whatever new name — will continue.

The IPA Styles domain is the map of the entire IPA family — every recognized sub style, organized by lineage. The IPA is not one beer but a genus of beers, all sharing a hop forward identity yet diverging in malt, yeast, strength, and clarity. This note is the hub: use it to navigate, and see the IPA Family Tree for the visual genealogy. [!note] How to read this domain Every style note carries a quick stats table (ABV, IBU, color, key hops, glassware). For side by side numbers across all styles, jump straight to the IPA Style Comparison Table.

The modern IPA splits into three trunks, each with its own descendants. 1. The British Lineage The original. Earthy, balanced, malt aware. English IPA — the ancestral style; biscuity malt, English hops. 2. The American Lineage The craft era reinvention that conquered the world. American IPA — the broad parent category. West Coast IPA — clear, dry, aggressively bitter, citrus pine. New England IPA — hazy, soft, juicy, low perceived bitterness. Double IPA — imperial strength, 8–10% ABV. Triple IPA — the 10%+ extreme tier. Session IPA — sub 5% ABV, hop forward but light. Red IPA — amber, caramel malt forward American IPA. 3. The Hybrid & Cross Genre Lineage IPAs that borrow from other beer families or fermentation traditions. Black IPA — dark malt meets hop intensity. White IPA — IPA crossed with witbier. Belgian IPA — Belgian yeast esters and phenols plus IPA hopping. Rye IPA — rye malt spice. Brut IPA — bone dry, champagne like. Milkshake IPA — lactose, fruit, and vanilla; full bodied. Cold IPA — lager yeast and adjuncts; crisp. Brett IPA — Brettanomyces funk plus hops. Sour IPA — kettle soured hoppy beer. India Pale Lager — the IPL; an IPA hopped lager.
Beyond the named styles lies a continuous frontier of novelty — Specialty and Experimental IPAs — where brewers blend, mutate, and invent faster than guidelines can catalog them.

Competitions and judges impose order on this chaos. The note BJCP and Style Guidelines explains how the Beer Judge Certification Program and others draw boundaries, assign vital stats ranges, and decide what counts as which style. [!tip] A moving target Read Modern IPA Diversification alongside this domain. The IPA family tree grows new branches every few years; any style map is a snapshot, not a final word.

This note places every major IPA style side by side. Numbers are typical commercial ranges drawn from BJCP and Style Guidelines and contemporary brewing practice — individual beers routinely stray outside them. [!note] How to use these ranges ABV is alcohol by volume; IBU is IBU and Perceived Bitterness; SRM measures color (low = pale, high = dark). "Haze" and "mouthfeel" are sensory judgments, not lab values.

Style ABV % IBU SRM (color) Haze Key Hops Malt Character Mouthfeel English IPA 5.0–7.5 40–60 8–14 amber gold Clear English (Noble and English Hops\) Biscuity, toasty Medium, balanced American IPA 5.5–7.5 40–70 6–14 gold amber Clear–light Cascade, Centennial, Citra Light, supportive Medium, dryish West Coast IPA 6.0–7.5 60–100 5–10 pale gold Brilliant Simcoe, Centennial, Cascade Very lean, dry Light, crisp, bitter New England IPA 6.0–7.5 25–60 (low perceived) 4–8 pale Heavy, opaque Citra, Mosaic, Galaxy Soft, oat/wheat Pillowy, full Double IPA 8.0–10.0 60–120 6–14 Varies High oil American Modest but present Medium full, warming Triple IPA 10.0–13.0+ 70–120+ 6–14 Varies Heavy late/dry hop Restrained, sweet edge Full, hot, viscous Session IPA 3.0–5.0 30–55 3–8 pale Varies Aroma forward American Minimal Light, thin crisp Black IPA 5.5–9.0 50–90 25–40 dark Clear Citrus pine American Dehusked dark malt Medium, smooth White IPA 5.5–7.0 40–70 4–7 pale Hazy Citrus American Wheat, coriander/orange Light, spritzy Belgian IPA 6.2–9.5 50–100 5–14 Varies American or noble Light Effervescent, dry Rye IPA 5.5–8.0 50–90 7–14 Clear–light American Rye spice, dry grain Medium, slick Red IPA 6.0–8.5 50–90 11–19 amber red Clear Citrus pine American Caramel, crystal Medium full Brut IPA 6.0–7.5 20–35 2–5 very pale Clear–light Aroma forward None perceptible Bone dry, effervescent Milkshake IPA 6.0–8.0 20–50 4–10 Heavy, opaque Tropical American Lactose sweetness Thick, creamy Cold IPA 6.0–7.5 55–80 3–6 pale Brilliant Simcoe, Citra Lean, corn/rice adjunct Crisp, snappy Brett IPA 5.5–8.0 40–70 5–12 Varies American Light Dry, sometimes spritzy Sour IPA 5.0–7.5 10–40 4–9 Often hazy Tropical American Light Tart, crisp India Pale Lager 5.5–7.5 50–80 4–9 pale Clear American Clean, lean Crisp, lager smooth

[!tip] What the table reveals Bitterness is diverging. Once an IPA's defining trait, IBU now ranges from 10 (Sour IPA) to 120+ (Triple IPA). Haze is a style axis of its own. Compare the brilliant West Coast IPA with the opaque New England IPA — see Hop Haze and Colloidal Stability. Strength spans a triple range , from 3% sessionable to 13% extreme.

The English IPA is the ancestral style — the beer from which the entire modern family descends. It is the British pale ale that, in the 19th century, travelled to colonial India and lent the genus its name. Compared with its American descendants it is restrained, malt aware, and built around the earthy, floral character of English hops rather than tropical exuberance. [!example] Quick stats ABV 5.0–7.5% IBU 40–60 Color 8–14 SRM (golden to light amber) Key hops Noble and English Hops\ Glassware Nonic pint or English tulip pint

The English IPA grew out of the well hopped, well attenuated pale ales of Burton on Trent and Burton Pale Ale and the export trade described in Hodgson and the East India Trade. The romantic notion that brewers loaded the beer with hops purely to survive the voyage is partly The October Beer Myth — strong, hoppy stock ales already existed — but the India trade did push a particular pale, bitter, well aged ale into fame. By the late Victorian era "IPA" was a recognized commercial category across Britain.

Aroma — earthy, floral, herbal, sometimes lightly marmalade; far quieter than an American IPA. Hop oils here are low in citrus terpenes. Malt — a defining feature, not an afterthought. British pale and crystal malts give biscuit, light toast, and a gentle nutty caramel. Bitterness — firm but rounded; English Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio (Burton water) sharpens it without harshness. Yeast — English ale strains contribute mild fruity esters (pear, light stone fruit) and a touch of minerality. See IPA Yeast Strains.

Trait English IPA American IPA Hop character Earthy, floral, herbal Citrus, pine, tropical Malt presence Prominent, biscuity Light, supporting Balance Toward balance Toward hops Carbonation Moderate Moderate–high Serving temp Cellar cool, 10–12°C Cooler, 7–9°C [!tip] Brewing the style Use English base and crystal malts, a clean English ale yeast, and an all English hop bill. Treat brewing liquor toward Burton's sulfate profile. Resist the urge to dry hop heavily — restraint is the point.

The English IPA faded at home through the 20th century, eclipsed by mild and bitter and later by lager — a story told in Decline of IPA in Britain. It survives today both as a living British tradition and as a deliberate "throwback" brewed by craft breweries worldwide.

The American IPA is the craft era reinvention of the style and the broad parent category from which the West Coast IPA and New England IPA descend. Where the English IPA is earthy and balanced, the American IPA is unapologetically hop forward: citrus, pine, and tropical fruit lifted high over a lean, clean malt base. It is the best selling craft style in the United States and the beer most drinkers picture when they hear "IPA." [!example] Quick stats ABV 5.5–7.5% IBU 40–70 Color 6–14 SRM (gold to light amber) Key hops Cascade, Centennial, Citra, Simcoe Glassware Shaker pint, IPA glass, or tulip

The American IPA emerged from The American Craft Beer Revolution of the late 1970s and 1980s. The pivotal beer was Anchor Liberty Ale and the First Modern IPA (1975), the first modern American ale to showcase the bright, grapefruit citrus Cascade hop. Through the 1990s breweries pushed hopping rates ever higher, and the style crystallized into a recognized category — see Rise of the West Coast IPA.

The defining ingredient is the American hop . Pacific Northwest varieties — grown across the Hop Growing Regions of Washington, Oregon, and Idaho — carry terpene and Thiols and Hop Burst profiles bursting with grapefruit, orange, pine resin, and tropical fruit. The malt bill is deliberately lean: pale base malt with only a touch of crystal, engineered to get out of the hops' way. See Base Malts and Hop Oils and Terpenes.

The American IPA is less a single recipe than a launchpad: Descendant Divergence from the parent West Coast IPA Drier, more bitter, brilliantly clear New England IPA Hazy, soft, juicy, low perceived bitterness Double IPA Scaled up to 8–10% ABV Session IPA Scaled down below 5% ABV Red IPA Built on an amber, caramel malt base

Aroma — assertive citrus, pine, resin, and tropical fruit. Flavor — hop flavor matches the aroma; bitterness is firm and clean. Finish — dry to medium dry, inviting the next sip. Yeast — typically a neutral American ale strain so hops dominate; see IPA Yeast Strains. [!tip] Freshness is everything American IPAs are at their peak within weeks of packaging. Hop aroma fades fast — see Hop Fade and Oxidation and IPA Freshness and Shelf Life.

The West Coast IPA is the classic American IPA taken to its bitter, lean, brilliantly clear extreme. It is the style that dominated craft beer through the 2000s and the benchmark against which the New England IPA later defined itself by contrast. Its identity is built on three pillars: aggressive bitterness, a bone dry finish, and crystal clarity. [!example] Quick stats ABV 6.0–7.5% IBU 60–100 Color 5–10 SRM (pale to deep gold) Haze Brilliant clarity Key hops Simcoe, Centennial, Cascade, Amarillo Glassware IPA glass or tulip

The style grew out of California and Pacific Northwest breweries in the 1990s, chronicled in Rise of the West Coast IPA. Brewers in hop country had ready access to fresh, high alpha Hops and pushed both bittering and aroma additions hard. The result became the defining craft beer of its era.

1. Bitterness Firm, clean, and lingering — often 60–100 IBU and Perceived Bitterness. A sulfate forward water profile sharpens the bitter edge; see Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio. 2. A Dry Finish Highly attenuated and lean. The grain bill is pale malt with minimal crystal, sometimes cut further with sugar or adjuncts to thin the body and dry the finish. See Recipe Formulation. 3. Clarity The West Coast IPA is brilliant . Brewers achieve it with proper Clarification and Haze Management and by avoiding the high protein grists and ultra late hop loads that cause haze.

Aroma — grapefruit, orange peel, pine, resin, and dank "catty" notes. Flavor — assertive hop flavor over a cracker dry malt base. Mouthfeel — light, crisp, almost spritzy — see The Science of Mouthfeel. Finish — long, dry, decisively bitter.

Trait West Coast IPA New England IPA Clarity Brilliant Opaque haze Bitterness High, assertive Low perceived Mouthfeel Lean, crisp Soft, pillowy Hop expression Pine, citrus, resin Juicy tropical Finish Dry, bitter Soft, round [!tip] The "modern West Coast" revival After years overshadowed by haze, the West Coast IPA returned around 2020 in a slightly updated form — still clear and dry, but with brighter, more tropical hop bills. See Modern IPA Diversification.

The New England IPA — also called the Hazy IPA or Juicy IPA — is the soft, opaque, tropical forward counterpoint to the West Coast IPA. Where the West Coast style prizes clarity and bitterness, the NEIPA prizes haze, a pillowy mouthfeel, and saturated juicy hop flavor with the bitterness pulled deliberately low. It is the most influential IPA development of the 2010s. [!example] Quick stats ABV 6.0–7.5% IBU 25–60 (low perceived bitterness) Color 4–8 SRM (pale straw to gold) Haze Heavy, opaque Key hops Citra, Mosaic, Galaxy, Sabro, Nelson Sauvin Glassware Tulip or stemmed IPA glass

The style emerged in Vermont and the wider Northeast in the early 2010s, a story told in The New England IPA Emergence. The Alchemist's Heady Topper and Hill Farmstead's beers are commonly cited as foundational. Within a decade the hazy IPA had spread worldwide and reshaped commercial expectations of the category.

NEIPA haze is intentional and structural — see Hop Haze and Colloidal Stability. It is engineered through: High protein grists — flaked oats and wheat alongside Base Malts. Massive late and dry hopping — hop polyphenols bind with proteins. Soft water — a chloride forward profile (see Water Chemistry and the Sulfate Chloride Ratio). Specific yeast — fruity, low flocculating English derived strains; see IPA Yeast Strains.

The signature "juice" comes from heavy late hopping and from Biotransformation — yeast enzymes converting hop compounds during fermentation — plus the release of aromatic Thiols and Hop Burst. The result is mango, pineapple, peach, and citrus with little of the pine or resin found in a West Coast IPA.

Element Character Appearance Opaque, glowing, orange juice like Aroma Saturated tropical and stone fruit Bitterness Low and soft — see IBU and Perceived Bitterness Mouthfeel Full, creamy, pillowy — see The Science of Mouthfeel Finish Round, soft, gently sweet [!warning] The freshness problem NEIPAs are extremely perishable. Their soft hop aromatics fade and oxidize within weeks, and haze can degrade — see Hop Fade and Oxidation and IPA Freshness and Shelf Life. Drink them as fresh as possible, cold.

The Double IPA — also called the Imperial IPA or DIPA — is the American IPA scaled up: 8–10% ABV, intensified hopping, and a malt base built to carry the extra weight without becoming sweet or heavy. It is a study in controlled excess, where the brewer's craft lies in making a very strong beer taste dangerously drinkable. [!example] Quick stats ABV 8.0–10.0% IBU 60–120 Color 6–14 SRM (gold to amber) Key hops High oil American — Simcoe, Citra, Mosaic, Centennial Glassware Tulip or snifter (smaller pour)

The Double IPA was born in California in the mid 1990s — its origin story is told in Origins of the Double IPA, with Russian River's Pliny the Elder the most cited landmark. It formalized "imperial" as a strength tier within the American IPA family and became a craft beer status symbol.

A Double IPA is not merely "an IPA with more of everything." Its difficulty lies in three balancing acts: 1. Hot alcohol vs. drinkability. Too much crystal malt or under attenuation leaves a cloying, boozy beer. Brewers often add simple sugar to boost ABV while keeping the body lean — see Recipe Formulation. 2. Bitterness vs. harshness. Huge bittering charges can turn coarse; careful Hop Additions and Timing and Whirlpool and Hop Stand shift emphasis toward flavor and aroma. 3. Hop saturation vs. cost. Double IPAs use enormous quantities of hops, raising both cost and the risk of Hop Creep and Refermentation.

The style now splits along the same haze axis as the standard IPA: Clear (West Coast) DIPA Hazy (NE style) DIPA Bitterness High, assertive Lower, soft Mouthfeel Lean, dry, warming Full, creamy Hop expression Pine, citrus, resin Juicy tropical See West Coast IPA and New England IPA for the parent expressions.

Aroma — intense and layered: citrus, tropical fruit, resin, dank. Flavor — concentrated hop flavor; bitterness firm but not punishing. Mouthfeel — medium to full with a gentle alcohol warmth. Finish — dry, despite the strength. [!warning] Respect the strength At 8–10% ABV a Double IPA delivers roughly twice the alcohol of a standard pint. Serve it in a smaller glass and treat it accordingly.

The Triple IPA is the extreme upper tier of the IPA family — beers of 10% ABV and beyond, brewed with the heaviest hop charges in craft. It is less a distinct flavor style than a strength category , an escalation of the Double IPA into territory where alcohol management becomes the central brewing problem. [!example] Quick stats ABV 10.0–13.0%+ IBU 70–120+ Color 6–14 SRM (gold to amber) Key hops Heavy late and dry hop charges; high oil American varieties Glassware Snifter or small tulip (modest pour)

The Triple IPA emerged as a logical extension of the imperial arms race that began with the Origins of the Double IPA. Russian River's Pliny the Younger is the most famous example. There is no firm boundary with the Double IPA — most guidelines, including BJCP and Style Guidelines, treat "triple" informally, simply as a Double IPA pushed past 10%.

At this strength the brewer fights physics and biology: Challenge Why it matters Common solution Fermenting to dryness Yeast struggles in high alcohol Healthy pitch, stepped sugar additions, Fermentation management Avoiding hot alcohol Fusel and solventy notes spike Controlled temps, simple sugars over crystal malt Hop saturation Diminishing returns, Hop Creep and Refermentation\ Massive but well timed Dry Hopping Drinkability Beer can turn syrupy A very lean grain bill — see Recipe Formulation [!tip] Sugar is the secret Triple IPAs lean heavily on simple sugars (dextrose, sucrose). These ferment fully, raising ABV without adding body — keeping a 12% beer from drinking like a barleywine. See Specialty Malts and Adjuncts.

Aroma — saturated hop intensity; the beer's strength is often barely masked. Flavor — concentrated hop flavor with an unavoidable alcohol presence. Mouthfeel — full and warming, sometimes viscous; see The Science of Mouthfeel. Finish — dry by design but with lingering heat. [!warning] Drink with caution A modest pour of a 12% Triple IPA contains as much alcohol as several standard beers. These are sipping beers, best shared.

The Session IPA is the IPA scaled down — a sub 5% ABV beer that keeps the hop forward aroma and flavor of an American IPA while shedding the alcohol and weight. The word "session" refers to a long drinking session: a beer light enough to enjoy several of without fatigue. It is the mirror image of the Double IPA. [!example] Quick stats ABV 3.0–5.0% IBU 30–55 Color 3–8 SRM (pale straw to gold) Key hops Aroma forward American — Citra, Mosaic, Amarillo Glassware Pint or IPA glass

The Session IPA rose to prominence in the early 2010s as a counterweight to the imperial escalation of the Origins of the Double IPA era. Founders All Day IPA is the most cited landmark. It answered a real demand: drinkers wanted hop character without a high ABV commitment.

A low ABV beer offers the brewer very little to work with. The difficulty is keeping a Session IPA from tasting thin, watery, or harshly bitter: Body — small additions of wheat, oats, or dextrin malt add mouthfeel; see Base Malts and The Science of Mouthfeel. Bitterness balance — with little malt sweetness, aggressive bittering turns coarse. Brewers shift hops to Whirlpool and Hop Stand and Dry Hopping additions for flavor over raw IBU and Perceived Bitterness. Aroma retention — the goal is maximum hop aroma per unit of alcohol. [!tip] Session IPA vs. Pale Ale The line is blurry. A Session IPA generally hops harder and finishes drier than an American pale ale of the same strength, leaning aroma forward rather than balanced.

Element Character Aroma Bright citrus and tropical fruit Flavor Clear hop flavor, modest malt Bitterness Moderate, clean Mouthfeel Light, crisp, sometimes delicate Finish Dry and quenching The Session IPA exists in both clear (West Coast IPA) and hazy (New England IPA) variants, following the same haze divide as the rest of the family.

The Black IPA — also known as the Cascadian Dark Ale (CDA) or India Black Ale — is one of the IPA family's most paradoxical members: a dark, roast tinged beer that nonetheless drinks with the hop intensity of an American IPA. The name itself is an oxymoron ("black pale ale"), and the style has carried a faint identity crisis its whole life. [!example] Quick stats ABV 5.5–9.0% IBU 50–90 Color 25–40 SRM (dark brown to black) Key hops Citrus pine American — Centennial, Cascade, Simcoe Glassware IPA glass or tulip

The style coalesced in the U.S. Pacific Northwest in the 2000s. The competing names reflect a genuine dispute: Northwest brewers preferred Cascadian Dark Ale to honor the region and the Cascade hop, while Black IPA won out commercially. BJCP and Style Guidelines eventually codified it (as "American style India Black Ale" or similar), settling the practical question if not the philosophical one.

The central brewing problem is achieving black color without the heavy roast bitterness of a stout, which would clash with hop bitterness. The solution is dehusked or debittered dark malts (such as Carafa Special or midnight wheat) — malts that contribute color but very little roast astringency. See Specialty Malts and Adjuncts. [!tip] Color without roast A well made Black IPA tastes like a hoppy IPA that happens to be black. If it tastes like a hoppy stout, the dark malt has been overused or the wrong type chosen.

Element Character Appearance Dark brown to black, often with garnet edges Aroma Citrus and pine hops, faint cocoa or coffee hint Flavor Hop forward with a light roast/chocolate undertone Bitterness Firm; hop and (mild) roast bitterness combined Mouthfeel Medium, smooth — see The Science of Mouthfeel

The Black IPA peaked around 2010–2013 and then faded as the New England IPA captured attention. It survives as a respected niche and a frequent winter seasonal — part of the Specialty and Experimental IPAs long tail.

The White IPA is a hybrid style: a Belgian IPA witbier crossed with an American IPA. It marries the pale, wheat driven body, coriander, and orange peel spicing of a wit with the citrus hop punch of an American IPA. The result is light, aromatic, and refreshingly spritzy. [!example] Quick stats ABV 5.5–7.0% IBU 40–70 Color 4–7 SRM (pale, hazy straw) Key hops Citrus American — Citra, Amarillo, Cascade Glassware Tulip or weizen glass

The White IPA appeared around 2010–2011, most famously as a collaboration between Boulevard Brewing and Deschutes. It was an early example of the collaboration and mashup era described in Modern IPA Diversification, deliberately splicing two well established families together.

Witbier contributes IPA contributes Wheat heavy grist, hazy pale body Aggressive late hopping Belgian wit yeast (clove, banana, pepper) Citrus forward American Hops Coriander and orange peel spicing A drier, more bitter finish The witbier yeast is the key cross over ingredient — its phenolic spice and fruity esters interact with citrus hop oils to amplify the orange and spice impression. See IPA Yeast Strains. [!tip] Spice with restraint Traditional wit spicing (coriander, bitter orange peel) can easily overwhelm hop aroma. The best White IPAs use a light hand, letting the yeast and hops carry most of the aromatic load.

Appearance — pale and naturally hazy from the wheat. Aroma — orange, lemon, coriander, light clove, soft citrus hops. Flavor — bready wheat, gentle spice, citrus hop flavor. Mouthfeel — light, soft, highly carbonated and spritzy. Finish — dry and quenching.
The White IPA sits among the IPA family's cross genre experiments alongside the Belgian IPA, the Rye IPA, and the Sour IPA. All take a non IPA beer tradition and graft IPA hopping onto it.

The Belgian IPA grafts Belgian yeast character onto an IPA hop bill. It keeps the bitterness and hop aroma of an American IPA but ferments with a Belgian ale or saison yeast, layering in the fruity esters and spicy phenols that define Belgian brewing. The result is an effervescent, dry, complex beer where yeast and hops compete for attention. [!example] Quick stats ABV 6.2–9.5% IBU 50–100 Color 5–14 SRM (gold to amber) Key hops American or noble — Cascade, Centennial, Noble and English Hops\ Glassware Tulip or Belgian goblet

The Belgian IPA arose in the 2000s from both directions: American craft brewers pitching Belgian yeast into IPAs, and Belgian brewers (such as those behind Houblon Chouffe and Urthel Hop It) hopping their strong ales more aggressively. It was an early entry in the cross genre wave of Modern IPA Diversification.

What separates a Belgian IPA from an American IPA is entirely the IPA Yeast Strains. Belgian ale and saison strains contribute: Esters — pear, apple, banana, stone fruit. Phenols — clove, white pepper, light spice. High attenuation — a very dry, sometimes spritzy finish. These yeast derived flavors interact with hop terpenes in unpredictable ways — sometimes harmonizing into a "tropical spice" character, sometimes clashing. See Hop Aroma Compounds. [!warning] A balancing act Belgian yeast esters and hop aromatics can fight each other. The best Belgian IPAs pick a hop bill that complements the yeast — earthy or noble hops with a phenolic saison strain, citrus hops with a fruity Belgian ale strain.

Element Character Aroma Hops plus pear, clove, pepper, light spice Flavor Hop flavor woven through fruity spicy yeast Bitterness Moderate to high, softened by dryness Mouthfeel Light, effervescent, dry — see The Science of Mouthfeel Finish Crisp, dry, sometimes warming
The Belgian IPA belongs to the cross genre branch of the family alongside the White IPA (a wit hybrid), the Brett IPA (wild yeast funk), and the Sour IPA.

The Rye IPA — sometimes written RyePA — is an American IPA in which a portion of the grain bill is replaced with rye malt. Rye adds a distinctive peppery, grainy spice and a slick mouthfeel, giving the beer an extra dimension of dryness and complexity that pairs naturally with citrus and resinous hops. [!example] Quick stats ABV 5.5–8.0% IBU 50–90 Color 7–14 SRM (gold to light amber) Key hops Citrus pine American — Simcoe, Centennial, Cascade Glassware IPA glass or pint

Rye is the defining ingredient — see Specialty Malts and Adjuncts. In modest proportions (typically 10–25% of the grist) it brings: Spice — a dry, peppery, faintly earthy grain note. Mouthfeel — a characteristic silky, slick texture; see The Science of Mouthfeel. Color and complexity — a slightly deeper hue and a drier finish. [!warning] The stuck mash hazard Rye has no husk and is rich in gummy beta glucans. Too much rye can produce a sticky, slow running mash — see Lautering and Sparging. Rice hulls and a careful Mashing keep the lauter moving.

sections.h: Why Rye and Hops Work Together
sections.t: Rye's peppery dryness amplifies the perception of hop bitterness and accentuates spicy, resinous hop notes. It pushes the beer toward a leaner, more savory profile than a malt sweet Red IPA — making rye a popular partner for pine and citrus hop bills.

Element Character Aroma Citrus and pine hops with a grainy edge Flavor Hop forward, dry, with peppery rye spice Bitterness Firm, sharpened by rye's dryness Mouthfeel Slick, medium, smooth Finish Notably dry and spicy
The Rye IPA is one of the IPA family's grain forward variations, a sibling to the Red IPA and the Black IPA — each defined less by yeast or fermentation than by a distinctive Malt choice.

The Red IPA is an American IPA built on an amber to red malt base. By adding crystal and caramel malts to the grain bill, the brewer gives the beer a deeper color and a layer of caramel, toffee, and toasted flavor — a malt backbone that the lean West Coast IPA deliberately avoids. It is the IPA family's most overtly malt forward member. [!example] Quick stats ABV 6.0–8.5% IBU 50–90 Color 11–19 SRM (amber to deep red) Key hops Citrus pine American — Centennial, Cascade, Simcoe Glassware IPA glass or tulip

The Red IPA evolved from the American amber ale — a hoppy amber pushed toward IPA strength and bitterness. BJCP and Style Guidelines recognizes it as a specialty IPA category. It sits at the intersection of two craft staples: the amber ale and the American IPA.

The defining feature is the use of crystal and caramel malts — see Specialty Malts and Adjuncts. These contribute: Color — the characteristic amber to garnet hue. Flavor — caramel, toffee, light toast, dried fruit. Body — a fuller, rounder mouthfeel than a standard IPA. [!warning] The cloying trap Crystal malt is unfermentable sugar. Too much leaves the beer sweet, sticky, and heavy — the opposite of a refreshing IPA. The art of the Red IPA is using just enough caramel character to support, not smother, the hops. See Recipe Formulation.

A caramel malt base pairs especially well with piney, resinous, and dank hops, which echo and contrast the toffee sweetness. Bright citrus hops also work but can read as marmalade against the malt. See Hop Oils and Terpenes.
